narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 51.8% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2 and 1 respectively, lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on those most predictive of bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which stands well below the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar; this low performance in your later undergraduate years signaled a concerning trend to the model. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 36 worked in your favor, as it pulled the prediction toward passing since it's close to the 37.27 average among successful bar takers, softening the overall risk assessment somewhat. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 provided a positive counterbalance, exceeding the 3.24 average for those who passed and helping to argue against failure. While your full-time enrollment plan aligns with the vast majority of passers (over 93%), and your family income bracket is represented among them (though less commonly than middle or upper-middle incomes), these played smaller roles compared to your grades. Overall, your profile shows strengths like a solid UGPA and decent LSAT, but the sharp drop to a bottom-decile Year 3 performance outweighed them, placing you below typical accepted students who maintain stronger trajectories throughout undergrad. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, which could shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying—your UGPA already gives you a great foundation to build on. We encourage you to seek advising from pre-law counselors who can help tailor a plan to your full-time goals and background. With targeted improvements, especially in later-stage performance, you could align more closely with profiles of those who pass the bar on their first attempt.
